coredata.dc:title: Amines-mediated β-glucose pentaacetate to generate photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots for visual monitoring the freshness of shrimp
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yaqin
Dong, Shengye
Chen, Jiaxin
Gao, Wenli
Shi, Xingbo
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a portable fluorescence-based functional hydrogel loaded with β-d-glucose pentaacetate (β-D-GP) is designed for high-sensitive quantification of amine vapor and visual monitoring of freshness of shrimp. We found for the first time that amine vapor can mediate β-D-GP to generate photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots (PCNDs) with good optical properties. On this basis, a functional hydrogel sensing platform is simply formed by solidifying β-D-GP in agarose hydrogels. When exposure to the volatile amines released from the spoilage of shrimp, β-D-GP in hydrogel is immediately mediated by amines to generate PCNDs, resulting in obvious fluorescence-based color variation of functional hydrogel. Notably, a smartphone is used to obtain digital photographs and RGB (Red/Green/Blue) information of hydrogels for on-site quantitative analysis. The gray value of G/(R + B) of hydrogel shows good linearity with trimethylamine (TMA) vapor concentration in the range of 0–59.49 × 10−9 mol dm−3. More importantly, the G/(R + B) value of functional hydrogel is successfully used to assess the freshness of shrimp. Consequently, this strategy provides a low-cost, portable fluorescence analysis device with promising applications in achieving high-sensitive, nondestructive, and on-site food safety evaluation of animal-derived aquatic products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer-carbon nanodots
Volatile amines
Functional hydrogel
Smartphone
Freshness of shrimp

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Generation of PCNDs by TMA-mediated β-D-GP
Acquisition and processing of fluorescence-based colorimetric information
The generation of PCNDs by TMA-mediated β-D-GP
Feasibility of TMA detection by functional hydrogel
Optimization of parameters for functional hydrogel system
Visual quantification of TMA vapor by functional hydrogel
Application of functional hydrogel in detection of shrimp spoilage

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV–vis absorption (left) and fluorescence (right) emission spectra (the inset photograph is under daylight and under white light). (b) TEM (inset was the size distribution histogram), (c) XPS and (d) C1s, (e) N1s, and (f) O1s spectra of the PCNDs.
Fluorescence spectra of (1) β-D-GP, (2) TMA, (3) β-D-GP + TMA in gel systems. Inset is the corresponding photograph.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of functional hydrogel under different TMA vapor concentrations. The concentrations of TMA vapor from 1 to 13 are 0, 17.84 × 10−9, 22.30 × 10−9, 29.73 × 10−9, 44.61 × 10−9, 59.49 × 10−9, 89.28 × 10−9, 111.60 × 10−9, 148.90 × 10−9, 178.80 × 10−9, 358.60 × 10−9, 721.10 × 10−9, and 1788.00 × 10−9 mol dm−3, respectively. (b) The relationship of the ratio of G/(R + B) versus different concentrations of TMA vapor, and the inset is a fitted linear relationship.

Visual monitoring of shrimp freshness by the functional hydrogel when the samples were stored at 37 °C. (a) Color evolution photos of samples and hydrogels, and (b) corresponding ratio value changes of G/(R + B) of hydrogel. (c) The changes of TVB-N detected by the semimicro-Kjeldahl method and the proposed method in monitoring the freshness of shrimp. (d) Correlation of the results between the two methods. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Schematic of shrimp freshness monitoring by the smartphone-integrated hydrogel sensing platform loaded with PCNDs.

head.ce:title: Amines-mediated β-glucose pentaacetate to generate photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots for visual monitoring the freshness of shrimp

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety
Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology
College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Laboratory of Micro & Nano Biosensing Technology in Food Safety, Hunan Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Science and Biotechnology, College of Food Science and Technology, Hunan Agricultural University, Changsha, 410128, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a portable fluorescence-based functional hydrogel loaded with β-d-glucose pentaacetate (β-D-GP) is designed for high-sensitive quantification of amine vapor and visual monitoring of freshness of shrimp. We found for the first time that amine vapor can mediate β-D-GP to generate photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots (PCNDs) with good optical properties. On this basis, a functional hydrogel sensing platform is simply formed by solidifying β-D-GP in agarose hydrogels. When exposure to the volatile amines released from the spoilage of shrimp, β-D-GP in hydrogel is immediately mediated by amines to generate PCNDs, resulting in obvious fluorescence-based color variation of functional hydrogel. Notably, a smartphone is used to obtain digital photographs and RGB (Red/Green/Blue) information of hydrogels for on-site quantitative analysis. The gray value of G/(R + B) of hydrogel shows good linearity with trimethylamine (TMA) vapor concentration in the range of 0–59.49 × 10−9 mol dm−3. More importantly, the G/(R + B) value of functional hydrogel is successfully used to assess the freshness of shrimp. Consequently, this strategy provides a low-cost, portable fluorescence analysis device with promising applications in achieving high-sensitive, nondestructive, and on-site food safety evaluation of animal-derived aquatic products.

ce:section.ce:para: Food safety issue caused by the spoilage of animal-derived aquatic products such as shrimp has gained widespread public attention [1–3]. Due to their richness in proteins, endogenous enzymes, and psychrophilic microorganisms, animal-derived aquatic products are prone to spoilage [4,5], thereby resulting in not only great financial losses but also serious human health damage. To avoid the unintentional consumptions of the spoiled animal-derived aquatic products, many traditional techniques have been used to evaluate their freshness, including quality index method (QIM) [6,7], high-performance liquid chromatography method (HPLC) [8–10], hyper spectral imaging method (HIS) [11–13] and microbial inspection method [14,15]. However, most of these conventional techniques require complex sample pretreatments, which are time-consuming, cumbersome, and destructive, resulting in the inability to achieve highly sensitive, on-site, and non-invasive detection.

Recently, the novel sensor technology has been used to replace traditional technologies for the freshness evaluation of shrimp [16–19], due to their low-cost, simplicity, rapid response, and practicability, etc. Particularly, the sensor strategies based on the analysis of indicators of food spoilage show a good application prospect [20,21]. Volatile amines are one of the most widely used indicators since they are usually generated from the decomposition of protein-rich food [22]. Trimethylamine (TMA) is one of representative volatile amine, which is usually chosen as an important biomarker to monitor the freshness of animal-derived aquatic products [23,24], because of not only its produced by the conversion of trimethylamine oxide (TMAO) in food but also its high saturated vapor pressure.

Fluorescent biosensors have received extensive concern for their high sensitivity in the food safety [25,26]. Recently, smartphone is used to acquire color information on the fluorescence-based colorimetric methods, making it more accurate, sensitive, and portable. Especially, the color information is digitized by the RGB (Red, Green, and Blue) analysis, thereby deducting the fluorescence intensity variation through the three-channel ratio [27]. As a result, smartphone-based fluorescence sensors are widely involved in precise assessment of food safety, such as the detection of food contaminants [28,29], pathogens [30,31], toxins [32,33], allergens [34,35], and the evaluation of food spoilage [36,37].

Carbon nanomaterials are remarkable fluorescent probe for portable biosensing because of their low toxicity, cost-effective, and resistance to photobleaching [38,39]. Generally, carbon nanomaterials are mainly synthesized by hydrothermal or solvothermal under rigorous conditions such as high temperature and pressure. Nevertheless, the syntheses of photoluminescent polymer-carbon nanodots (PCNDs), are reported mainly by mild method, which are attracted much attention [40–42]. PCNDs are mainly obtained by incomplete carbonization of polymer clusters and are usually prepared from monomers or non-conjugated polymers through assembly, condensation, cross-linking, or micro-carbonization processes [41]. It integrates both the remarkable optical properties of conventional carbonized carbon dots and the polymers properties. Especially, with a large number of surface groups and polymer chains, PCNDs are easily enriched with target molecules or ions and have been used to construct various sensors [43,44]. Since amines are used to facilitate the synthesis of N-doped PCNDs with rich polymer structures and low carbonization [45,46]. That gives an opportunity for the synthesis of amine mediated PCNDs, further developing a new method for monitoring the freshness of shrimp.

Herein, we design a portable fluorescence functional hydrogel with PCNDs generated by amines mediating β-d-glucose pentaacetate (β-D-GP) (as show in Scheme 1 ). On this basis, the smartphone-integrated fluorescence functional hydrogel was applied to high sensitively quantify amines vapor and visual monitor the freshness of the shrimp. The volatile amines released from the spoilage of food enter the hydrogel through diffusion, and directly react with the precursors β-D-GP in the hydrogel to generate fluorescence PCNDs in situ. The fluorescence intensity of hydrogel enhanced gradually with the increase of amines vapor concentration, making it available for monitoring of the shrimp freshness. The fluorescence information of hydrogel can be digitized by integrating smartphone with RGB analysis, avoiding the usage of sophisticated instrumentation. Importantly, in comparison of previously reported methods, the functional hydrogel is easily fabricated by only solidifying β-D-GP in agarose hydrogel and circumvents the synthesis of signal probe in advance. As a result, the proposed strategy provided a cost-effective, portable method to achieve high-sensitive, nondestructive, and on-site monitoring of food freshness.

β-d-glucose pentaacetate (β-D-GP) was obtained from J&K Scientific Ltd (Beijing, China). Ethanol and Quinine sulfate dihydrate ((C20H24N2O2)2·H2SO4·2H2O) were purchased from Sinopharm Group (Shanghai, China). Biowest agarose was bought from New England BioLabs Inc. (Ipswich, UK). Trimethylamine (TMA, 30 wt% in water), dimethylamine (DMA, 40 wt% in water), ethylenediamine (ELA), tryptamine (TPA), histamine (HTA), β-phenylethylamine (β-PA, 98 wt% in water), tyramine (TRA) and trichloroacetic acid were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. (Shanghai, China). All the chemical reagents were used without any further purification. Prawn (Metapenaeusensis) samples were bought from the local market and processed in the laboratory within an hour.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Generation of PCNDs by TMA-mediated β-D-GP
ce:section.ce:para: PCNDs were generated using β-D-GP as the carbon source and TMA as the co-reactant. Briefly, β-D-GP (0.098 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of ethanol to form a homogeneous suspension solution under sonication. Then TMA (30 wt %, 10 mL) was added to the above solution, and the resultant mixture was reacted at 40 °C for 20 min. Finally, the solution was dialyzed in dialysis bag (500–1000 D) for 12 h. The obtained PCNDs were freeze-dried for further characterization and use. And the fluorescence quantum yield (QY) was measured and calculated according to the established procedure [38].

The morphology of PCNDs was taken via a JEM-ARM200F field-emission transmission electron microscopy (FE-TEM, FEI, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were carried out on a Nicolet 50 infrared spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). To confirm the structure, the X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks were collected on an XRD-6000 diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was taken by ESCALAB Xi+ XPS instrument (Thermo Fischer, American). Photographs of the functional hydrogel were collected by a Huawei P30 smartphone with auto focus (Huawei, China). For optical properties investigation, Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a multifunctional microplate reader (Spark, TECAN).

Agarose (40 mg) was completely dissolved in ultrapure water (4 mL) after microwave heating for 30 s. Subsequently, 1 mL of β-D-GP (30 mM) was added, and then the substances were mixed by heating in microwave oven for 20 s. Then, 80 μL of the mixed solution was quickly transferred into a PE (polyethylene) tube cap while hot. After drying at room temperature for 1 min, the hydrogel was solidified, indicating that the functional hydrogel was formed. The functional hydrogel was stored in a 4 °C refrigerator before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Acquisition and processing of fluorescence-based colorimetric information
ce:section.ce:para: The functional hydrogel was placed in a fixed position and ensured that ultraviolet lamp (365 nm, 15 W) was used for homogeneous illumination. A “Huawei P30” mobile with tri-lens camera (40 MP + 16 MP + 8 MP) was utilized to take the fluorescence-based colorimetric images. The smartphone was placed on a holder, keeping a proper distance (15 cm) from the functional hydrogel. The images were imported into the ImageJ for image processing, and then a certain area (30 × 30 pixel2) was selected to extract the average value of the R, G, and B channel intensities. Finally, the ratio of G/(R + B) model was used to output the image information.

The detection of volatile TMA using the functional hydrogel is illustrated in previous work [16,17,47]. Typically, 200 μL of TMA with various vapor concentrations of 0, 17.8 × 10−9, 22.3 × 10−9, 29.7 × 10−9, 44.6 × 10−9, 59.5 × 10−9, 89.3 × 10−9, 111.6 × 10−9, 148.9 × 10−9, and 178.8 × 10−9 mol dm−3 were added into the PE tubes with a functional hydrogel in the PE tube cap, and sealed for reaction about 20 min. Finally, the functional hydrogels were transferred into a 96-well plate, then the fluorescence emission spectra from 425 to 600 nm were recorded (under 395 nm excitation). For quantitative detection, the photographs of functional hydrogels were collected by a smartphone. RGB analysis was performed by the ImageJ, and the ratio value of G/(R + B) was utilized for quantitative assay.

Shrimp (Metapenaeusensis) was chosen as the actual sample for the experiment. Both sample and functional hydrogel were placed in a plastic bowl (350 mL) without contact. In practical application, the cap of bowl was sealed so that all the TMA generated during the spoilage of shrimp sample can be collected. The samples were monitored under the storage temperatures of 37 °C.

According to the national standard (GB5009.228-2016), the freshness of shrimp samples with the same storage conditions was also assessed by the semimicro-Kjeldahl method. The TVB-N content of the shrimp samples (20 g) was determined by distillation and titration during visual monitoring.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The generation of PCNDs by TMA-mediated β-D-GP
ce:section.ce:para: Amines have been reported to mediate the generation of N-doped PCNDs with excellent optical properties [45,46]. Inspired by this fact, TMA, a representative indicator of food spoilage, was used to react with the carbon source β-D-GP at 40 °C for 20 min to generated PCNDs for the first time. The synthesis procedure is explained in the Experimental Section (Fig. S1). The solution of PCNDs shows a clear yellow color in daylight and bright-blue fluorescence under UV-light (inset of Fig. 1 a). The absorption peak at around 390 nm corresponds to n–π* transition of multi-conjugate CO (Fig. 1a), which is common properties of PCNDs. [48,49]. Note that the PCNDs solution produces a fluorescence peak at 485 nm under excitation at 365 nm, and the fluorescence QY is 26.1% (with quinine sulfate as a reference). Fig. 1b shows the TEM image of PCNDs, revealing that the nanodots are spherical particles and have uniform dispersion, with an average size of 1.09 ± 0.15 nm. The XRD spectrum shows that the PCNDs has a broad graphite phase characteristic diffraction peak in the range of 2 Theta of 20–30° (Fig. S2), which is attributed to the amorphous nature of the synthesized PCNDs.

The surface composition and element analysis of the resultant nanodots were characterized by the FT-IR spectrum and XPS spectrum. The FT-IR results show obvious structural differences between the precursor β-D-GP and generated PCNDs (Fig. S3). For β-D-GP, the absorption bands at 1750 cm−1 can be assigned to the CO asymmetric stretching vibrations of the ester, and the peak at 1232 and 1067 cm−1 are ascribed to the asymmetric stretching vibration of C–O–C group. After reaction with TMA, the characteristic absorption bands of aliphatic C–N at 1157 cm−1, and N–H at 1639 and 3468 cm−1, reveals that the local surface structure of the reaction product is composed of C–N units. And the absorption around 3468 cm−1 is attributed to the overlap of stretching vibrations from –OH and –NH2 groups, suggesting that TMA is involved in the reaction and mediates the β-D-GP reaction to produce –OH during the generation of PCNDs [50,51]. Moreover, the oxygen-containing functional groups such as C–O–C and CO still are present in the PCNDs, revealing that the synthesized PCNDs have excellent water solubility.

The surfaces of PCNDs were further investigated by XPS spectrum. The full spectra (Fig. 1c) show three typical peaks: C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (400.8 eV), and O1s (531.8 eV), indicating that these nanodots are mainly composed of C (67.1%), N (4.5%), and O (28.4%) elements. The XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 1d) core level electrons of PCNDs exhibits four fitted peaks at 284.6, 285.4, 286.1, and 287.8 eV, which are associated with C–C, C–N, C–O, and CO/CN groups, respectively. The N1s Spectra (Fig. 1e) exhibit two peaks at 399.5 and 401.6 eV, which are attributed to C–N–C and N–H bands, respectively (the additional peak at 403.4 eV is related to the reduction of nitrates under the X-ray beam). The two peaks at 531.2 and 532.7 eV in O1s spectrum (Fig. 1f) are attributed to CO and C–OH/C–O–C groups, respectively. The C1s and N1s spectra obviously show the formation of aliphatic-like N and amino-like N in the PCNDs, confirming the nitrogen element was successfully incorporation into the PCNDs. Therefore, the nitrogenous surface group confirmed that TMA participate in the reaction of PCNDs formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of TMA detection by functional hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Owing to the remarkable optical properties of the PCNDs mediated by TMA, we further explored the feasibility of using such PCNDs for TMA detection. The preparation of the hydrogel and its response process to TMA are shown in Fig. S4. First, the agarose hydrogels loaded with β-D-GP were prepared in PE tube caps. The functional hydrogel then trapped trace TMA vapor released from the bottom liquid in the sealed PE tube system, thereby generating PCNDs in situ on the hydrogel with a strong fluorescence emission peak at 485 nm (Fig. 2 b). In addition, with the increase of reaction time, the fluorescence intensity of hydrogel enhanced gradually (Fig. S5). A plateau was gradually achieved when the reaction time reached 30 min, indicating that the β-D-GP is completely reacted by TMA vapor in the hydrogel, and that it is credible to use the functional hydrogel for monitoring the freshness of shrimp at 1-h intervals. Meanwhile, as illustrated in Table S1, the hydrogels can almost maintain their original response properties for at least a month when kept at 4 and 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters for functional hydrogel system
ce:section.ce:para: Since the β-D-GP and agarose concentration can affect the optical properties of generated PCNDs, the hydrogel system was optimized to obtain the best conditions for sensing analysis. As shown in Figure S6a-S6b, the fluorescence intensity shows an increasing trend with the increase of β-D-GP concentration. However, the excessive concentration of β-D-GP would affect the preparation of hydrogel because of the poor solubility of β-D-GP. Therefore, the maximum concentration of 6 mM dissolved in ethanol under reaction temperature was selected as the optimum concentration for subsequent experiments. Similarly, as the concentration of agarose increased, the fluorescence signal also increased accordingly (Fig. S6c-S6d). When the agarose concentration reached 0.8%, the fluorescence intensity plateaued. To ensure the best solidification state of hydrogel, the most suitable concentration for gelation is 0.8% agarose. As a result, we determined the optimal reaction conditions for the subsequent sensing hydrogel system, namely 6 mM β-D-GP and 0.8 wt% agarose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual quantification of TMA vapor by functional hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Notably, the functional hydrogel integrated with a smartphone simplifies signal acquisition and digital data processing, making it an effective platform for portable on-site detection [52]. Under optimal conditions, we investigated the analytical performance of the smartphone-integrated functional hydrogel for the quantification of TMA vapor. Different concentrations of TMA solutions were added into the sealed microcentrifuge tubes so that the functional hydrogel fixed on the cap could capture the TMA vapor with different concentrations. Fig. 3 a shows the fluorescence intensity variance of functional hydrogel with the concentration of TMA vapor in the range of 0–3771.4 × 10−9 mol dm−3. Higher concentrations of TMA induce higher concentrations of generated PCNDs, thereby resulting in higher fluorescence intensity of the hydrogels at 485 nm. Furtherly, a smartphone was used to obtain RGB information of hydrogels for on-site quantitative analysis. A remarkable linear correlation can be observed in the plots of the ratio value of G/(R + B) versus the concentration of TMA vapor from 0 to 59.49 × 10−9 mol dm−3 (Fig. 3b). The limit of detection (LOD) for TMA vapor is estimated to be 1.4054 × 10−9 mol dm−3 at the signal-to-noise ratio of 3. As shown in Table S2, the LOD of the proposed method is comparable than that of most of those previous reported, indicating well analytical performance and potential application.

The analytical performance of functional hydrogel was also assessed in terms of recovery rates and relative standard deviations (RSDs). As shown in Fig. S7, the recoveries are 101.14%, 101.04%, 99.39%, 90.30% and 106.04% for the concentrations of TMA vapor of 0, 17.84 × 10−9, 22.30 × 10−9, 29.73 × 10−9, 44.61 × 10−9, 59.49 × 10−9 mol dm−3, respectively. In addition, the RSDs in the range of 0.65%–1.95% are obtained. These results indicate good accuracy and reproducibility of the proposed method for the quantification of TMA.

Since the spoilage of shrimp can form many other nitrogenous compounds other than TMA during metabolic decomposition, we investigated the fluorescence response of several other amines including TMA, DMA, ELA, TPA, HTA, β-PA and TRA and mixed amines. As shown in Fig. S8, it is mainly TMA and DMA that caused the fluorescence response of the hydrogels, and they are also the main volatile amines produced during the spoilage of shrimp [22]. On this basis, the hydrogels are practical for monitoring the freshness of shrimp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of functional hydrogel in detection of shrimp spoilage
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of smartphone-integrated functional hydrogel, we used shrimp (Metapenaeusensis) as an example to continuously monitor its freshness. Shrimp samples were placed in a plastic bowl and sealed with a functional hydrogel to simulate quantitative packaging (Fig. S9). The fluorescence intensity of functional hydrogel indicates the degree of spoilage of shrimp, which is related to the temperature and time of storage. Fig. 4 a shows the monitoring of sample freshness at 37 °C for 10 h. The images of hydrogel under UV lamp were acquired at intervals of 1 h (B of Fig. 4a). The response signal (G/(R + B)) value of the functional hydrogel increased sharply after 5 h (Fig. 4b). However, the G/(R + B) value of the blank group hydrogel showed no significant change. These results suggest that the response signal from RGB analysis is practicable for the monitoring of shrimp spoilage.

Since the freshness of shrimp samples is affected by temperature and storage time, we investigated the visual monitoring of shrimp freshness under the storage temperature 25 °C and 4 °C. The response signal (G/(R + B)) values of functional hydrogels increased sharply at 6 h storage at 25 °C and 3 days storage at 4 °C (Fig. S10 and S11). In addition, we also monitor the shrimp freshness at different shrimp sample weights and package sizes. As shown in Fig. S12 and S14a, the higher the shrimp sample mass is, the more the formation of volatile amines is, thus triggering a higher G/(R + B) value for a fixed volume of functional hydrogel response. For the same weight of shrimp samples, larger space volumes hold lower volatile amine concentrations, and resulting in a weaker fluorescence response (Fig. S13 and S14b). To improve the practicality of the method in routine applications, standard weights and package sizes of shrimp samples should be suggested, such as 30 g shrimp sample in 350 mL plastic bowl for 80 μL functional hydrogels.

The validity of this method was further confirmed by comparison with the semimicro-Kjeldahl method, which is commonly used as authoritative standard method to quantify the TVB-N content. According to the Chinese national standard (GB2733-2015), shrimp products containing TVB-N more than 30 mg/100 g are not permitted to be consumed and marketed. As shown in Fig. 4c, the TVB-N content in fresh shrimp is about 11.97 mg/100 g. After stored at 37 °C for 6 h, the TVB-N content rapidly increased to 34.43 mg/100 g, indicating that the shrimps were already inedible. At the same time, the G/(R + B) value of functional hydrogel increased obviously, which also indicates that the shrimp have been totally spoiling. Fig. 4d illustrates that the G/(R + B) value evolution of functional hydrogel is completely consistent with the results of the standard TVB-N measurement, and the linear relationship with Pearson correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9861) between two methods can further confirm the validity of proposed method. The calculated G/(R + B) value according to this method is about 0.3918, corresponding to a TVB-N content of 30 mg/100 g, indicating that the shrimp was spoiled. These results indicate that the freshness of shrimp can be detected conveniently and accurately by the smartphone-integrated functional hydrogel. Taking advantage of this property, the functional hydrogel possesses great potential practical application for on-site and non-invasive visual monitoring the shrimp freshness in the supply chain.

In summary, we designed a portable fluorescence-based functional hydrogel loaded with β-D-GP, which was successfully performed to achieve highly sensitive, non-invasive, and on-site monitoring of the freshness of shrimp. Since amine mediated the reaction of β-D-GP to generate fluorescent PCNDs with remarkably optical properties, it could be used as fluorescent probes for the quantification of amine. Further, the functional hydrogel was simply solidified by only adding β-D-GP in the agarose hydrogels. In the presence of amine vapor, PCNDs were generated in the hydrogel and exhibited a strong blue fluorescence. To quantify the intensity variation, the fluorescence color information was recorded via smartphone and then digitized by RGB analysis. The G/(R + B) value of hydrogel was successfully used to not only detect TMA vapor but also visual monitor the freshness of shrimp. More importantly, the accuracy and practicality of this simple, low-cost, and potable method was validated by using the TVB-N standard detection method. This proposed strategy is of great significance in food safety supervision and well present enormous potential application in food industry.

Yaqin Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Shengye Dong: Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Jiaxin Chen: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yating Li: Methodology, Formal analysis. Wenli Gao: Formal analysis, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Xingbo Shi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge that this work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC, 31972155), Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2020RC3050, 2022JJ10028), Key Research and Development Program of Hunan Province (2021NK2021), Training Program of Excellent Young Innovators of Changsha (kq2106048).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2022.123706.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biogenic amines: quality index of freshness in red and white meat
Food Control

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Vinci, M. Antonelli, Biogenic amines: quality index of freshness in red and white meat, Food Control 13 (2002) 519-524.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A high performance fluorescent arylamine sensor toward lung cancer sniffing
Sensor. Actuator. B Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Shang, G. Wang, M. He, X. Chang, J. Fan, K. Liu, H. Peng, Y. Fang, A high performance fluorescent arylamine sensor toward lung cancer sniffing, Sens. Actuators B Chem. 241 (2016) 1316-1323.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Development of a smart packaging for the monitoring of fish spoilage
Food Chem.
